coredata.dc:title: The facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for developing a powerful “on-off-on” fluorescence probe to detect glutathione in vegetables
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xinyang
Wang, Chao
Li, Peng
Shao, Zhiying
Xia, Ji
Liu, Qin
Shen, Fei
Fang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: To determine the glutathione (GSH) content in vegetables, an “on-off-on” fluorescence probe was developed by a synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using the microwave pyrolysis considering citric acid and L-cysteine as precursors. The fluorescence of N,S-CDs was quenched by adding Cu2+ at a concentration of 20–200 μmol/L due to the inner filter effect. The quenched fluorescence of N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system was recovered by adding the GSH at a concentration of 10–150 μmol/L due to the sulfhydryl-metal compound mechanism. By observing the GSH concentrations measured by our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system vs. a traditional fluorescent chelating method, the two measurements provided the GSH data with a good consistence by showing the RSD range of 1.86%–2.27%. This indicates the validation and novelty of our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system as being a powerful fluorescent probe for effectively and efficiently determining the GSH in vegetables.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S co-doped carbon dots
“On-off-on” probe
Glutathione
Vegetables

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Article(s) from the Special Issue on 5th International Symposium on Phytochemicals in Medicine and Food by Shao-Ping Nie and Jiaoyan Ren Ren

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of GSH in a variety of vegetables

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), HR-TEM image (B), size distribution (C, inset: AFM image) and FTIR spectrum (D) of synthesized N, S-CDs.
XPS spectra of N, S-CDs (A) and the high-resolution peaks for C1s (B), N1s (C), O1s (D) and S2p (E).

(A) UV–vis spectra of N, S-CDs (blue curve: the excitation spectra of N, S-CDs at the wavelength of 360 nm where the maximum fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs was obtained; red curve: the emission spectra of N, S-CDs at the maximum fluorescence intensity; inset left: N, S-CDs under white light, inset right: N, S-CDs under the wavelength of 360 nm for UV light); (B) Fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs for the emission spectra over a wide range of excitation wavelengths of 300–400 nm with a 20 nm increment. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence response, linear range and selectivity of Cu2+ to N, S-CDs. (A) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs solution at various concentrations of Cu2+(from top to bottom: 0 to 500 μmol/L); (B) A linear relationship of F/F0 against the Cu2+ addition from 20 to 200 µM; (C) Fluorescence response of N, S-CDs to various metal ions at the concentration of 300 μmol/L.

Fluorescence response, linear range and selectivity of GSH to N, S-CDs@Cu2+. (A) Fluorescence spectra of a N, S-CDs@Cu2+ system solution at various concentrations of GSH; (B) A linear relationship of F/F0 against the GSH addition from 10 to 150 μmol/L; (C) Fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@Cu2+ to various acids at the concentration of 300 μmol/L.
The concentration of GSH in various vegetables measured by a traditional fluorescent chelating method vs. N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system.

head.ce:title: The facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for developing a powerful “on-off-on” fluorescence probe to detect glutathione in vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Finance and Economics/Collaborative Innovation Center for Modern Grain Circulation and Safety, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering
Nanjing University of Finance and Economics/Collaborative Innovation Center for Modern Grain Circulation and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Finance and Economics/Collaborative Innovation Center for Modern Grain Circulation and Safety, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To determine the glutathione (GSH) content in vegetables, an “on-off-on” fluorescence probe was developed by a synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using the microwave pyrolysis considering citric acid and L-cysteine as precursors. The fluorescence of N,S-CDs was quenched by adding Cu2+ at a concentration of 20–200 μmol/L due to the inner filter effect. The quenched fluorescence of N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system was recovered by adding the GSH at a concentration of 10–150 μmol/L due to the sulfhydryl-metal compound mechanism. By observing the GSH concentrations measured by our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system vs. a traditional fluorescent chelating method, the two measurements provided the GSH data with a good consistence by showing the RSD range of 1.86%–2.27%. This indicates the validation and novelty of our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system as being a powerful fluorescent probe for effectively and efficiently determining the GSH in vegetables.

ce:section.ce:para: Current analytical techniques, i.e., high-performance liquid chromatography (Mcdermott et al., 2011), electrochemical methods (Ngamchuea, Lin, Batchelor-Mcauley, & Compton, 2017), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Ngamchuea, Batchelor-Mcauley, & Compton, 2017), mass spectrometry (George, Zachariadis, & Rosenberg, 2013), spectrofluorimetry (Yan et al., 2016), magnetic resonance imaging (Zhao, Fan, Zhou, Bai, & Tan, 2014) and colorimetry (Ni et al., 2015), have been used for detecting the Glutathione (L-γ-glutamyl-L-cysteinyl-glycine, GSH). Although most of these techniques are sensitive and accurate, each of them still has some drawbacks as being time consuming, requiring expensive instrumentation, and having a complicated operation procedure. Therefore, the fast, accurate and low-cost analytical methods are in a great need for GSH detection (Bavatharani et al., 2020; Khan, Wabaidur, Alothman, Busquets, & Naushad, 2016; Lakshmi, Wabaidur, Alothman, & Ragupathy, 2020; Siddiqui, Alothman, & Rahman, 2017; Wabaidur et al., 2016). Fluorescence probes based on a variety of fluorescence sensing platforms that possess the advantages of simplicity, low cost and high sensitivity, have been widely used (AlFaris et al., 2020; Wabaidur, Eldesoky, & Alothman, 2018).

Carbon dots (CDs) with relatively small particle size (<10 nm), are a new fluorescent nanomaterial that has attracted growing interest in analytical chemistry over the last decade (Liu et al., 2018). Compared to the conventional semiconductor quantum dots that usually contains heavy metals, the CDs have a stable luminescence, good biocompatibility and low toxicity (Borse, Thakur, Sengupta, & Srivastava, 2017). This makes the CDs as being a powerful tool for bioimaging (Liu, Tang, Deng, Cao, & Qiu, 2019), photocatalysis (Xue, Zhang, Zhan, Zou, & Zhao, 2016), fluorescence sensors (Qu et al., 2013), and optronics (Shen et al., 2017). The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was significantly increased by nitrogen doping (Liu et al., 2018). This indicates that N-CDs tend to serve as a fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of Cr (VI) ions due to the fluorescence quenching effect of Cr (VI) ions (Luo et al., 2018). However, the determination of GSH in vegetables using the CDs has not been reported yet.

In this research, we present a fast and low-cost strategy for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using a microwave pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and L-cysteine (L-Cys) precursors. The objectives of this research were to demonstrate a potential use of the synthesized N,S-CDs as a selective and sensitive “on-off-on” probe for determining the GSH in a variety of vegetables, and also to characterize the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs using Cu2+. We were aiming to prove a good capability of our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system being as a fluorescence probe to accurately quantify the GSH in the vegetables over a wide range of varieties.

The analytical reagents, i.e., CA, L-Cys, quinine sulfate (QS), O-Phthalaldehyde (OPA), Cu2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Na+, GSH, arginine (Arg), valine (Val), proline (Pro), glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), histidine (His), ascorbic acid (AA), tartaric acid (TA), cysteine (Cys), HCl, and H2SO4, that is used in this research without further purification, were provided by the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Vegetable samples, i.e., cucumber, sweet potato, tomato, pimiento, carrot, were purchased from a commercial market. The criterion for sample collection was that among the vegetables in the market, a relatively higher concentration of GSH was seen in our selected samples that have a putative ranking of GSH concentration: cucumber > sweet potato > tomato > pimiento > carrot. Deionized water with a conductivity of 18 MΩ cm−1 was purified by a Millipore water system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, U.S.A.).

N,S-CDs were obtained using a one-step microwave pyrolysis strategy with some minor modifications (Liu, Xiao, Gong, Wang, & Ye, 2014). The CA and L-Cys were dissolved in deionized water via ultrasonication, resulting in a uniform and transparent solution. This solution was then subjected to the microwave-assisted pyrolysis using a microwave oven at 800 W. After the reaction flask being cooled down at the room temperature, the deionized water was added to dissolve the residues in the solution. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. This purified N,S-CDs was stored at 4 °C for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, SPI 3800 N, Seiko, Japan) were used to characterize the morphology of N,S-CD nanocomposites. The size of N,S-CDs was determined using a ZS-90 Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, Worcestershire, UK). The functional groups and chemical compositions of N,S-CDs were measured using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). FTIR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer with the KBr as a sample matrix (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, U.S.A.). XPS analyses were conducted using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, U.S.A.). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi High-Technologies, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi High-Technologies, Tokyo, Japan), whereas the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was determined using the QS as a reference (Kumar et al., 2016).

Due to the mechanism of fluorescence quenching and recovery, the prepared N,S-CDs were used to detect the Cu2+ and GSH. 1 mL of N,S-CDs was prepared in the deionized water. The fluorescence emission spectra of Cu2+ at the concentration of 0–500 μmol/L were recorded over the wavelength range from 370 to 600 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence spectra of GSH were examined when the GSH at the concentration of 0–300 μmol/L was added to the N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system. The selectivity of N,S-CDs towards Cu2+ and GSH was determined when a variety of metal ions and amino acids were added to the N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system. All the measurements were performed in triplicate.

The extraction of GSH from the vegetables, i.e., cucumber, sweet potato, tomato, pimiento, carrot, was carried out according to the method that is reported by Yang, Zhang, Zhang, Wang, and Choi (2019). The edible part of vegetables was mashed, and then mixed with deionized water. This vegetable-water mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The vegetable extract was stored at 4 °C. The levels of GSH in vegetable extracts were determined according to fluorescence spectra analyses in the Section 2.4. The data of GSH fluorescence spectra was then compared with that is measured using a traditional method like fluorescent chelating (Kand’ár, 2007).

The microwave time, and the ratio of raw materials and initial volume, were optimized during the preparation of N, S-CDs. This is illustrated in supplementary material (Sup. 1 and 2). When the microwave time (at a sequence of 100 s, 110 s, 120 s and 130 s) was 120 s, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was maximized (Sup. 1A). The fluorescence QY accounted for approximately 29% of fluorescence standard substance (Sup. 1B). For the QS, the fluorescence quantum yield accounted for approximately 54% of fluorescence standard substance at 360 nm (Sup. 1B). This is due to the long microwave times causing the carbon dots to agglomerate and quench. We have observed that an increase in the microwave time contributes to the production of carbon bots that agglomerate as a group, and in turn the fluorescence intensity of carbon bots starts quenching. The maximum fluorescence intensity (Sup. 1C) and fluorescence QY (Sup. 1D) were obtained at a molar ratio of raw materials of 1:1 (group: 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:1.5, 1:2). The fluorescence intensity (Sup. 1E) and the fluorescence QY (Sup. 1F) had the peaks at an initial volume of 20 mL (group: 10 mL, 20 mL, 30 mL).

The stability of CDs was tested by considering the effects of storage time, pH and ionic strength. The effect of storage time on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs is shown in Sup. 2A. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs remained constant for at least 300 min. Sup. 2B shows the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs over a range of pH 1–13. When the solution was strongly acidic (pH < 3) or alkaline (pH > 10), the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was significantly reduced. This is due to the protonation or deprotonation of surface groups like carboxyl and amino. Sup. 2C illustrates the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs in the NaCl at a concentration of 0–2 mol/L. The N,S-CDs were stable over a wide concentration range of salt ion solutions. This indicates that the ionic strength has quite a little effect on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs, demonstrating that our carbon dots like the N,S-CDs, had a good tolerance to high-salt conditions (Ye et al., 2017).

To characterize the N,S-CDs, its morphology, surface functional groups and optical properties were demonstrated by showing the TEM image, HR-TEM image, size distribution and FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1 ). The N,S-CDs exhibited a quasi-spherical shape (Fig. 1A). The graphitic core arrangement of carbon atoms had a planar lattice spacing of 0.22 nm by HRTEM (Fig. 1B). This finding is consistent with that is reported by Jin, Kim, Jun, Hong, and Jeon (2013). The N,S-CDs had a size distribution of 0.6–2.8 nm, with an average diameter of 1.6 nm (Fig. 1C). The AFM morphology measurement also matched the TEM observations. The FTIR absorption bands measured at 3417 cm−1 and 3367 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively (Fig. 1D). The absorption peak at 2899 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CH, whereas the absorption peak at 1607 cm−1 was assigned to the CO functional group (Alothman et al., 2020; Wabaidur et al., 2020). The peaks at 1559 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 were due to stretching vibrations of CN and CN, respectively (Alothman et al., 2020; Wabaidur et al., 2020). The presence of CS bonds was confirmed by the characteristic peak at 1020 cm−1 (Shi et al., 2017). The above FTIR results showed that there were abundant N-, S-, and O-containing active groups on the surface of N,S-CDs.

To obtain more detailed results, XPS was used to verify the characteristic functional groups of N-CDs. The characteristic binding energy signals of C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p exhibited four main peaks at 284.5 eV, 400.5 eV, 532.1 eV and 165.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 A). This has been confirmed by Yan et al. (2016). At a high resolution, the spectra of C1s were decomposed into three peaks at 284.5 eV (CC), 286.2 eV (C-OH) and 287.9 eV (COOH), respectively (Fig. 2B). The spectrum of N1s demonstrated three peaks for the three different N species, i.e., pyridine N, CNC, and graphitic N-C3 (Fig. 2C). These three peaks were located at 398.4 eV, 399.3 eV and 401.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 2C). The O1s spectrum peaks at 530.7 eV, 531.9 eV and 533.1 eV confirmed the presence of C-OH, COC and CO, respectively (Fig. 2D). The high-resolution spectrum of S2p obtained by XPS was fitted for the two peaks at 168.6 eV and 170.3 eV (Fig. 2E). This is corresponding with the presence of CS and C-SOX, respectively. The results shown by XPS spectra were consistent with that from FTIR analyses.

The optical properties, including the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CDs were illustrated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3 ). The absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs showed two characteristic peaks at 235 nm and 359 nm, with a tail extending into the visible range (black line, Fig. 3A). The peak located at 235 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bond (Li et al., 2018). This does not induce a strong fluorescence signal. The peak located at 359 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO bond. This produces a strong blue emission.

The N,S-CDs showed a strong emission centred at approximately λ = 440 nm (red line, Fig. 3A) when it was excited at 360 nm (blue line, Fig. 3A). The aqueous solution of N,S-CDs was transparent in daylight (inset left, Fig. 3A) and exhibited a strong blue colour under UV light at 360 nm (inset right, Fig. 3A). To characterize the optical properties of N,S-CDs, the emission spectra were measured at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of N,S-CDs reached its maximum value at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 3B). However, the peak of fluorescence emission at 440 nm did not change. This is due to an excitation-independent fluorescence behaviour that comes from t the uniform size of N,S-CDs. This finding is in agreement with the results from morphology characterization (Yang et al., 2018).

Due to the presence of many oxygen-containing functional groups, the N,S-CDs were expected to be useful for Cu2+ detection. Fig. 4 A illustrates the fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CD aqueous solution at various concentrations of Cu2+. A gradual decrease in the fluorescence signal at 440 nm was observed as the concentration of Cu2+ increased (Fig. 4B). The dependence of F/F0 against the increase in Cu2+ concentration was determined by showing the fluorescence intensity (F/F0) at 440 nm with Cu2+ addition (inset right, Fig. 4B). A linear response of F/F0 to the Cu2+ addition was observed over the Cu2+ concentration of 20–200 µM (inset right, Fig. 4B). This is an ideal performance of N,S-CDs that is able to measure the trace amount of Cu2+. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was 30.6 µM in terms of 3δ/m (AlFaris, Wabaidur, Alothman, Altamimi, & Aldayel, 2020). Where δ is the standard deviation of blank signal, and m is the slope of linear calibration curve (AlFaris, Wabaidur et al, 2020b). The selectivity of fluorescence probe for Cu2+ detection was evaluated by determining the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs to various other ions. Fig. 4C showed that the addition of Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. It also demonstrated that N,S-CDs had a sensitive and selective fluorescence detection for Cu2+ only. This indicates a potential use of N,S-CDs for Cu2+ detection in practical fields.

The sulfhydryl groups in GSH were able to form the complexes with metal ions (Leverrier, Montigny, Garrigos, & Champeil, 2007). In terms of this property, a novel detection model for GSH was established using the N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system. The quenched fluorescence of N,S-CDs@Cu2+ was regained with an increase in the GSH concentration. This indicates that the N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system has a potential use for GSH detection via a fluorescent “on-off-on” sensing platform. The addition of GSH had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. With increasing the GSH concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system increased (Fig. 5 A). The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system had a good linear relationship with the increase in GSH concentration of 10–150 μmol/L (Fig. 5B, R2 = 0.9984). In terms of N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system, the limit of detection (LOD) for GSH detection was 3.74 μmol/L. To evaluate the selectivity of N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system to GSH detection, the sensing effects of amino acids (i.e., Arg, Val, Pro, Gly, Ser, His) and organic acids (i.e., AA, TA, Cys) on the fluorescence response of N, S-CDs@Cu2+ were illustrated in Fig. 5C. The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs@Cu2+ system was not significantly affected by these acids, indicating a good selectivity of this system for GSH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH in a variety of vegetables
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments were carried out to demonstrate the practical applications of the N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system for measuring the GSH concentration in a variety of vegetables, i.e., cucumber, sweet potato, tomato, pimiento and carrot. A traditional fluorescent chelating method using the OPA as a complexing agent and our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system were both conducted for determining the concentration of GSH in a variety of vegetables. This is represented in Table 1 . The concentration of GSH in vegetable samples that is measured using our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system was consistent with that is measured using a traditional fluorescent chelating method (p > 0.05). By comparing the results from the two measurements, the RSDs ranged from 1.86% to 2.27% (Table 1). This indicates that our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system is a powerful tool demonstrating its great capability for accurately quantifying the GSH in the vegetables over a wide range of varieties (Luo et al., 2018).

An “on-off-on” probe was developed by a synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using the facile microwave pyrolysis. The synthesized N,S-CDs were used for determining the concentration of glutathione (GSH) in a variety of vegetables. The fluorescence of synthesized N,S-CDs was recovered by adding the GSH due to the sulfhydryl-metal compound mechanism. The stable fluorescence of synthesized N,S-CDs was quenched by adding the Cu2+ due to an inner filter effect. As such, we established the N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system as being a robust, validate and precious analytical method for the GSH detection. For example, our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system had an adequate selectivity and sensitivity for accurately quantifying the GSH in the vegetables over a wide range of varieties, i.e., cucumber, sweet potato, tomato, pimiento, and carrot. By observing the GSH concentrations measured by our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system vs. a traditional fluorescent chelating method, the two measurements provided the GSH data with a good consistence that was shown by the RSD range of 1.86% to 2.27%. This ensures the validation and accuracy for the GSH measurements by our N,S-CDs@Cu2 + system. Compared to the traditional fluorescent chelating method, our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system with a utilization of highly fluorescent N,S-CDs, had a better performance for effectively and efficiently detecting a trance amount of GSH. This indicates the novelty of our N,S-CDs@Cu2+ system that contributes to develop an accurate and fast analytical method for determining the GSH in vegetable matrices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Key Research and Development Program Jiangsu Province (BE2021370), the Earmarked Fund for Jiangsu Agricultural Industry Technology System (JATS [2020] 467), the National Youth Talent in Grain Area Support Program of China (LQ2018301) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131142.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
Effects of microwave time (A, B), ratio of raw materials (C, D) and initial volume (E, F) on fluorescence intensity and QYs of N, S-CDs during the synthesis process.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of storage time (A), pH (B) and ionic strength (C) on the stability of N, S-CDs.
